title: WITHDRAWAL OF LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT and TERMINAL EXTUBATION in the ICU for a 74-Year-Old Man with DEVASTATING ANOXIC BRAIN INJURY After a Prolonged Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest Who Is NOT Brain Dead and Has NO Written Advance Directive, with a FAMILY CONFLICT Between His Spouse-Surrogate and Adult Son: Confirming the Legal SURROGATE Hierarchy, Deciding by SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (What the Patient Would Want) Rather Than the Family's Wishes, Distinguishing Withdrawal of TREATMENT From Euthanasia (Withholding = Withdrawing; Allowing Natural Death; Doctrine of Double Effect), Using the ETHICS COMMITTEE for Conflict and Medical Futility, and Carrying Out a COMFORT-FOCUSED Terminal Extubation (DNR, Stop Nonbeneficial Interventions, Titrated Opioids/Benzodiazepines + Antisecretory Agents, Family Presence, Chaplaincy, Bereavement, and OPO Referral) WITHOUT Coercion or Patient Abandonment
subtitle: End-of-life decision-making in the ICU is one of the highest-stakes ETHICAL and LEGAL scenarios a nurse navigates. This case follows H. Whitfield, a 74-year-old man who suffered an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with a prolonged (~25-minute) downtime and is now ICU Day 7 after completing targeted temperature management and rewarming. MULTIMODAL NEUROPROGNOSTICATION performed at least 72 hours after rewarming shows DEVASTATING ANOXIC BRAIN INJURY — absent pupillary and corneal reflexes, status myoclonus, bilaterally absent N20 responses on somatosensory evoked potentials, a malignant EEG pattern, and a markedly elevated neuron-specific enolase — indicating an extremely poor neurologic prognosis. CRITICALLY, he is NOT BRAIN DEAD: he retains some brainstem function and intermittently over-breathes the ventilator, so this is a VALUES-BASED GOALS-OF-CARE decision, NOT a declaration of death (distinct from the brain-death/organ-donation case [[case-107]] and the post-arrest neuroprognostication case [[case-083]]). He remains on escalating norepinephrine and continuous renal replacement therapy for acute kidney injury, with multiorgan failure and no purposeful response. He has NO written advance directive, but he repeatedly told his wife over the years that he 'never wanted to be kept alive on machines with no chance of recovery.' His wife (his legal surrogate as spouse) believes he would want to be allowed to die naturally, while their adult son insists the team 'do everything.' The nurse must (1) CONFIRM DECISION-MAKING AUTHORITY — the patient lacks capacity, so the legally recognized SURROGATE HIERARCHY applies (a healthcare agent/POA if one exists, otherwise spouse, then adult children, then parents, then siblings per state law) and the surrogate speaks FOR the patient, not for themselves; (2) anchor the decision in SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT — what WOULD this patient choose, based on his known values and prior statements — using the BEST-INTERESTS standard only when wishes are truly unknown; (3) understand that WITHDRAWING life-sustaining treatment is ethically and legally permissible, that WITHHOLDING and WITHDRAWING are ethically equivalent, and that this ALLOWS THE UNDERLYING DISEASE to cause death — it is NOT euthanasia or physician-assisted death (the intent is comfort and the cause is the disease), and that titrating comfort medications under the DOCTRINE OF DOUBLE EFFECT is appropriate even if a foreseen-but-unintended side effect is a hastened death; (4) use the ETHICS COMMITTEE to help resolve the family conflict and clarify MEDICAL FUTILITY rather than allowing any one clinician to decide unilaterally; and (5) once a comfort-focused goal is agreed, carry out a COMFORT-FOCUSED TERMINAL EXTUBATION — change code status to DNR, discontinue nonbeneficial interventions (vasopressors, CRRT, routine labs, vital-sign checks, and alarms; stop tube feeds per goals), PRE-MEDICATE for comfort with titrated opioids and benzodiazepines and an antisecretory agent (glycopyrrolate/scopolamine) for terminal secretions, provide family presence, chaplaincy, and bereavement support, and make an organ/tissue-donation referral to the OPO per policy. Throughout, the nurse ADVOCATES and supports the surrogate WITHOUT imposing personal values or coercing the family, never frames the plan as 'withdrawing care' or 'nothing more we can do' (we withdraw nonbeneficial TREATMENT while intensifying COMFORT), and may exercise CONSCIENTIOUS OBJECTION to participating but must NOT abandon the patient and must ensure continuity of care. Step-6 evaluates whether each action is APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

patient.name: H. Whitfield
patient.code: Full Code on admission — under active reconsideration this shift (no written advance directive on file)
patient.admitDate: 7 days ago — out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with prolonged (~25-minute) downtime and bystander CPR; resuscitated to ROSC, completed targeted temperature management and rewarming. Now intubated/ventilated in the medical ICU on norepinephrine and continuous renal replacement therapy with multiorgan failure. Multimodal neuroprognostication ≥72 hours after rewarming indicates devastating anoxic brain injury with an extremely poor prognosis; he is NOT brain dead. No written advance directive; his wife is the legal surrogate, and an adult son disagrees with her about goals of care.
patient.room: Medical ICU, Bed 4; palliative care, chaplaincy, social work, and the ethics committee available in-house; wife and adult son at bedside

vitals.spo2: 94% on FiO2 0.60; sedation minimized for serial neuro exam; GCS 3 (E1 VT M1); absent pupillary + corneal reflexes; status myoclonus; CRRT running for anuric AKI; no purposeful response

93% on FiO2 0.60; physiologically unchanged and critical; intensivist shares the devastating prognosis with the wife and son; nurse, chaplain, social work present; conversation reframed around HIS values and prior statements

vitals.time: ICU Day 7, 1500 — comfort-focused plan agreed; ethics consult had been offered
vitals.spo2: 92% on FiO2 0.60; substituted-judgment decision reached (allow natural death, as he had clearly stated he would want); code status changed to DNR; comfort-care/withdrawal plan and anticipatory symptom orders written

vitals.bp: not actively measured per comfort plan (cuff/alarms off)
vitals.spo2: monitor and alarms discontinued per comfort plan; norepinephrine and CRRT stopped; routine labs/vitals discontinued; titrated morphine + midazolam given and glycopyrrolate for secretions; patient calm, no grimacing or restlessness; family at bedside with chaplain

vitals.rr: 14, occasional shallow agonal-pattern breaths without distress
vitals.spo2: not monitored; endotracheal tube removed, airway suctioned and mouth care given; comfort medications titrated to relieve any sign of air hunger (double effect); no grimacing, tachypnea, or accessory-muscle use; family present, holding his hands

vitals.time: ICU Day 8, ~1300 — peaceful death with family present
vitals.spo2: not applicable; died comfortably ~3.5 hours after extubation with no signs of distress; comfort goal met; family supported by chaplain and bereavement services; death pronounced and post-mortem care + bereavement follow-up arranged; tissue-donation eligibility had been screened with the OPO

labs.n: WHO decides — capacity, the healthcare agent, and the legal SURROGATE hierarchy
labs.v: Because Mr. Whitfield lacks decision-making CAPACITY (he cannot understand, appreciate, reason, or communicate a choice), someone must decide FOR him. The order is: (1) the patient's own contemporaneous wishes if he had capacity; (2) a healthcare AGENT named in a durable power of attorney for healthcare / healthcare proxy, if one exists; (3) absent a named agent, the DEFAULT SURROGATE HIERARCHY set by state law — typically SPOUSE first, then ADULT CHILDREN, then PARENTS, then SIBLINGS. He has NO written advance directive and no named agent, so his WIFE is the legal surrogate. A surrogate's job is to speak FOR THE PATIENT — to convey what HE would choose — not to substitute the surrogate's own preferences. The adult son does NOT outrank the spouse in the hierarchy and cannot unilaterally override her (unless state law or a valid document says otherwise).
labs.r: Capacity assessed (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate); decisional patient decides → else healthcare AGENT/POA → else default surrogate hierarchy (spouse → adult children → parents → siblings) per state law. The surrogate speaks FOR the patient, not for themselves.

labs.n: SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT vs the BEST-INTERESTS standard; resolving family conflict
labs.v: For an adult who once HAD capacity, the primary standard is SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT: decide as the PATIENT would have decided, using his known values, beliefs, and prior statements — here, his repeated, consistent statements that he 'never wanted to be kept alive on machines with no chance of recovery.' Those statements are strong evidence and should ANCHOR the decision. The BEST-INTERESTS standard (weighing benefits/burdens from a reasonable-person view) is used ONLY when the patient's wishes are genuinely unknown. When the wife and son disagree, the nurse refocuses the conversation away from 'what do YOU want for him' toward 'what would HE have wanted' — and offers an ETHICS CONSULTATION to mediate. The son's distress is real and deserves support, but his wish to 'do everything' cannot override the patient's own clearly expressed values conveyed by the legal surrogate.
labs.r: Substituted judgment (what the PATIENT would choose, from his values/prior statements) is primary for a once-capacitated adult; best-interests only when wishes are unknown. Anchor on his consistent statements; mediate family conflict by refocusing on HIS values + offering an ethics consult.

labs.n: Withdrawal of TREATMENT is NOT euthanasia — withholding = withdrawing; allowing natural death; DOUBLE EFFECT
labs.v: Withdrawing (or withholding) life-sustaining treatment from a patient — or a surrogate acting on his behalf — who would not want it is ETHICALLY AND LEGALLY PERMISSIBLE and is recognized as such by professional codes and case law. WITHHOLDING and WITHDRAWING are ethically EQUIVALENT (it is no more permissible to never start a therapy than to stop one that is not achieving the patient's goals). Stopping the ventilator/pressors ALLOWS THE UNDERLYING DISEASE to cause death — this is ALLOWING NATURAL DEATH, NOT killing. It is categorically DIFFERENT from EUTHANASIA or physician-assisted death: in withdrawal the INTENT is to relieve suffering and honor wishes and the CAUSE of death is the disease, whereas in euthanasia the intent and cause are to end life. The DOCTRINE OF DOUBLE EFFECT permits giving opioids/benzodiazepines in the doses needed to relieve dyspnea and agitation even if a FORESEEN-but-UNINTENDED effect might be a hastened death — because the intent is comfort, the act (symptom relief) is good, and the good effect is not achieved BY MEANS of the bad one. There is no arbitrary dose 'ceiling' when titrating to genuine symptoms.
labs.r: Withdrawing/withholding LST a patient wouldn't want is permissible; withholding = withdrawing; stopping support ALLOWS the disease to cause death (not killing) and is NOT euthanasia/PAD (intent + causation differ). Double effect: titrate opioids/benzos to comfort even if death may be hastened — intent is symptom relief, no artificial ceiling.

labs.n: MEDICAL FUTILITY, the ETHICS COMMITTEE, and the NURSE'S role (advocacy, conscientious objection, NO abandonment)
labs.v: MEDICAL FUTILITY should be used carefully: PHYSIOLOGIC/quantitative futility (an intervention cannot achieve its physiologic aim) is distinct from VALUE-LADEN 'qualitative' judgments about whether an outcome is 'worth it' — the latter belong to the patient/surrogate, not to clinicians alone. Persistent conflicts (clinician–family or family–family) are referred to the ETHICS COMMITTEE / consultation service for a fair, transparent process — NOT settled by any single clinician unilaterally stopping treatment. The NURSE's role: ADVOCATE for the patient's voice and values; ensure the surrogate's decision is INFORMED (prognosis, options, what each choice involves); coordinate and support the interdisciplinary family meeting; provide honest, compassionate, consistent information; and support the family emotionally and spiritually. A nurse who has a moral objection to participating in withdrawal may exercise CONSCIENTIOUS OBJECTION and withdraw from PARTICIPATION — but must do so transparently and IN ADVANCE, arrange a competent replacement, ensure CONTINUITY of care, and NEVER ABANDON the patient or stop comfort care.
labs.r: Distinguish physiologic futility from value judgments (the latter belong to patient/surrogate); send conflicts to the ETHICS COMMITTEE, not unilateral clinician action. Nurse: advocate, ensure informed decision, coordinate + support the meeting. Conscientious objection allowed only with advance notice + replacement + continuity — never abandonment.

labs.n: HOW comfort-focused TERMINAL EXTUBATION is carried out + PERTINENT NEGATIVES (what NOT to do)
labs.v: Once a comfort-focused goal is agreed: change code status to DNR; DISCONTINUE nonbeneficial interventions (vasopressors, CRRT/dialysis, routine labs and vital-sign checks, monitors and ALARMS; stop artificial nutrition/tube feeds per the agreed goals). PRE-MEDICATE for comfort BEFORE and during withdrawal — titrated OPIOIDS (e.g., morphine) for dyspnea/pain and BENZODIAZEPINES (e.g., midazolam) for agitation/anxiety, plus an ANTISECRETORY agent (glycopyrrolate or scopolamine) for terminal secretions ('death rattle'); have PRN boluses ready and titrate to the relief of distress (air hunger, grimacing, tachypnea, accessory-muscle use). Either TERMINAL WEANING (gradual reduction) or one-step extubation may be used; remove the tube, provide suction and mouth care, and position for comfort. Maximize FAMILY PRESENCE, offer CHAPLAINCY and cultural/spiritual rituals, and provide BEREAVEMENT support; make an ORGAN/TISSUE-donation referral to the OPO per policy (notification only — clinicians do NOT make suitability judgments and the OPO approaches the family separately). PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT say 'we are withdrawing CARE' or 'there is nothing more we can do' (we withdraw nonbeneficial TREATMENT and intensify COMFORT, hygiene, and presence); did NOT equate withdrawal with euthanasia; did NOT withhold ordered opioids out of fear of hastening death; did NOT continue futile labs, vitals, or alarms; did NOT let the son override the legal surrogate and the patient's known wishes; did NOT coerce or guilt the family; and did NOT abandon the patient.
labs.r: DNR + stop nonbeneficial interventions (pressors, CRRT, labs/vitals/alarms, tube feeds per goals). Premedicate + titrate opioids/benzos + antisecretory before/around extubation; terminal weaning or one-step; family presence, chaplaincy, bereavement, OPO referral. NEGATIVES: never 'withdrawing care'/'nothing more,' never equate with euthanasia, never withhold comfort meds, never continue futile monitoring, never override the surrogate, never coerce or abandon.

labs.n: Multimodal neuroprognostication (≥72 h post-rewarming) — basis for the poor prognosis (NOT brain death)
labs.v: GCS 3 (E1 VT M1) off sedation; ABSENT pupillary and corneal reflexes; STATUS MYOCLONUS within 24 h; bilaterally ABSENT N20 cortical responses on somatosensory evoked potentials; MALIGNANT EEG pattern (suppression/burst-suppression); markedly elevated neuron-specific enolase. These CONCORDANT findings on multimodal testing performed ≥72 h after rewarming indicate devastating anoxic-ischemic brain injury and an extremely poor neurologic prognosis. IMPORTANTLY, he intermittently over-breathes the ventilator and retains some brainstem reflex activity on serial exam, so he does NOT meet BRAIN-DEATH criteria — this is a poor-prognosis, value-based goals-of-care decision, not a declaration of death.
labs.r: Concordant poor-prognosis markers (absent brainstem reflexes, status myoclonus, absent N20 SSEP, malignant EEG, high NSE) at ≥72 h; intact reflexes/purposeful response = good. He is NOT brain dead (spontaneous respiratory effort + some brainstem function) → goals-of-care decision, not death determination.

labs.v: 95 mcg/L (markedly elevated — supports severe anoxic neuronal injury; interpreted only as part of MULTIMODAL prognostication, never in isolation, and after excluding hemolysis)
4.5 mmol/L (elevated — ongoing hypoperfusion/shock with escalating vasopressor requirement)
3.8 mg/dL (elevated — anuric acute kidney injury requiring CRRT; one component of his multiorgan failure)
7.22 (metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/multiorgan failure despite maximal support)

nursesNote: ICU Day 7, 1530 — 74 y/o man, ICU Day 7 after a prolonged out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, intubated on norepinephrine 0.24 mcg/kg/min and CRRT for anuric AKI with multiorgan failure. Multimodal neuroprognostication ≥72 h after rewarming shows devastating anoxic brain injury (GCS 3 off sedation, absent pupillary/corneal reflexes, status myoclonus, bilaterally absent N20 SSEP, malignant EEG, NSE 95) — extremely poor prognosis. He is NOT brain dead: he intermittently over-breathes the ventilator and retains some brainstem function, so this is a values-based goals-of-care decision, not a declaration of death. No written advance directive; wife is the legal surrogate (spouse). She states he repeatedly said he 'never wanted to be kept alive on machines with no chance of recovery'; their adult son wants the team to 'do everything.' Confirmed the patient lacks capacity and identified the wife as legal surrogate per the hierarchy (no POA on file). Coordinated an interdisciplinary family meeting (intensivist, RN, chaplain, social work); prognosis shared compassionately; conversation refocused on what HE would want (substituted judgment), not on the family's preferences. Offered an ethics consultation for the wife–son conflict and to address futility. With support, the family reached a comfort-focused decision consistent with his prior statements: code status changed to DNR and a comfort-care/withdrawal plan with anticipatory opioid, benzodiazepine, and antisecretory orders was written. Plan for terminal extubation Day 8: stop norepinephrine, CRRT, routine labs/vitals, monitors and alarms; premedicate and titrate morphine + midazolam to comfort (double effect) with glycopyrrolate for secretions; family presence, chaplaincy, and bereavement support; tissue-donation eligibility screened with the OPO per policy. Explained we are withdrawing nonbeneficial TREATMENT while intensifying COMFORT — not 'withdrawing care.' Did NOT coerce the family, did NOT equate withdrawal with euthanasia, did NOT withhold comfort medication for fear of hastening death, did NOT continue futile monitoring, did NOT let the son override the legal surrogate and the patient's known wishes, and did NOT abandon the patient. Wife and son supported and updated.
providerOrders: GOALS OF CARE: confirm the patient lacks decision-making capacity; identify the legal surrogate (healthcare agent/POA if any, otherwise spouse per the default hierarchy); decisions to be made by SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT based on the patient's known values and prior statements

FAMILY MEETING: convene an interdisciplinary family conference (intensivist, bedside RN, chaplain, social work, ± palliative care) to share prognosis and elicit the patient's values; offer an ETHICS COMMITTEE consultation to help resolve the family conflict and address questions of medical futility
PROGNOSIS: communicate the multimodal neuroprognostication findings honestly and compassionately; clarify that the patient is NOT brain dead and that this is a values-based goals-of-care decision
CODE STATUS: upon a comfort-focused decision, change code status to DNR/DNAR and document the comfort-care/withdrawal plan
DISCONTINUE NONBENEFICIAL INTERVENTIONS: stop vasopressors and CRRT; discontinue routine labs, vital-sign checks, monitors, and alarms; stop artificial nutrition/tube feeds per the agreed goals

COMFORT MEDICATIONS: titrated OPIOID (morphine) for dyspnea/pain and BENZODIAZEPINE (midazolam) for agitation, with PRN boluses; titrate to relief of distress (air hunger, grimacing, tachypnea) under the doctrine of double effect — no arbitrary dose ceiling when symptoms warrant; ANTISECRETORY agent (glycopyrrolate or scopolamine) for terminal secretions

TERMINAL EXTUBATION: premedicate for comfort, then perform terminal weaning or one-step extubation; provide suction, mouth care, and comfort positioning; reassess and re-dose comfort medications continuously
FAMILY + SPIRITUAL SUPPORT: maximize family presence at the bedside; offer chaplaincy and culturally appropriate rituals; provide bereavement support before and after death

ORGAN/TISSUE DONATION: make a timely referral to the Organ Procurement Organization (OPO) per policy (notification only; do not make suitability judgments; the OPO approaches the family separately after decoupling)

STAFF SUPPORT: honor any conscientious objection to participation only with advance notice, a competent replacement, and assured continuity of care — the patient is never to be abandoned; offer team debrief after the death

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL clinically and ethically significant cues.

opts.text: Multimodal neuroprognostication ≥72 h after rewarming shows devastating anoxic brain injury (absent pupillary/corneal reflexes, status myoclonus, bilaterally absent N20 SSEP, malignant EEG, NSE 95) with an extremely poor prognosis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — concordant multimodal findings at the appropriate time establish the poor prognosis that frames the goals-of-care conversation.

opts.text: He is NOT brain dead — he intermittently over-breathes the ventilator and retains some brainstem function — so this is a values-based goals-of-care decision, not a declaration of death
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distinguishing a poor-prognosis decision from brain death (a separate legal determination) is essential to framing the ethics correctly.

opts.text: He has NO written advance directive, and his wife (spouse) and adult son disagree about what he would want
opts.rat: RELEVANT — the absence of a document plus family conflict makes identifying the surrogate and applying substituted judgment central.

opts.text: He repeatedly told his wife he 'never wanted to be kept alive on machines with no chance of recovery'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — consistent prior statements are strong evidence of his wishes and anchor a substituted-judgment decision.

opts.text: He remains on escalating norepinephrine and CRRT with multiorgan failure and no purposeful response on ICU Day 7
opts.rat: RELEVANT — the trajectory of multiorgan failure despite maximal support informs prognosis and the benefit/burden discussion.

opts.text: Because he is not brain dead, treatment must legally continue indefinitely until he meets brain-death criteria
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A non-brain-dead patient with a poor prognosis can have life-sustaining treatment withdrawn based on his wishes/surrogate decision — survival to brain death is not required.

opts.text: Because the wife and son disagree, the nurse should pick whichever option seems best and proceed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The nurse does not decide; the legal surrogate decides using substituted judgment, and persistent conflict goes to the ethics committee.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What do these facts mean ethically and legally?

opts.text: This is a values-based goals-of-care decision for an incapacitated adult — the surrogate decides by SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what HE would choose), not by her own preferences
opts.rat: CORRECT — substituted judgment is the primary standard for an adult who once had capacity.

opts.text: The legal surrogate hierarchy applies (spouse first, then adult children) — the son does not override the wife unless state law or a valid document says otherwise
opts.rat: CORRECT — default surrogate hierarchies rank the spouse ahead of adult children absent a named agent or contrary law.

opts.text: Withdrawing life-sustaining treatment is ethically and legally permissible and is NOT euthanasia — it allows the underlying disease to cause death
opts.rat: CORRECT — withdrawal allows natural death; intent and causation differ from euthanasia/physician-assisted death.

opts.text: An ethics consultation is appropriate to help resolve the family conflict and clarify futility, rather than a unilateral decision by any one clinician
opts.rat: CORRECT — ethics committees provide a fair, transparent process for intractable conflict.

opts.text: Titrating opioids/benzodiazepines to relieve dyspnea and agitation is appropriate even if a foreseen, unintended effect could be a hastened death (doctrine of double effect)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the intent is symptom relief; comfort is the duty and there is no arbitrary dose ceiling when symptoms warrant.

opts.text: Since he can't speak for himself, the team should decide based purely on the clinicians' view of futility, disregarding his prior statements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. His known values and prior statements govern via substituted judgment; value judgments about a 'worthwhile' outcome belong to the patient/surrogate, not clinicians alone.

opts.text: Withdrawing the ventilator is the same as actively euthanizing the patient and is illegal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Withdrawal of unwanted treatment is legal and ethically distinct from euthanasia; withholding and withdrawing are ethically equivalent.

opts.text: Confirm the patient lacks capacity and identify the legal surrogate (healthcare agent/POA if any, otherwise spouse per the hierarchy)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Establishing who is authorized to decide is the foundation for everything that follows.

opts.text: Convene an interdisciplinary family meeting to share prognosis compassionately and elicit the patient's values
opts.rat: #2 — Bring the team and family together to inform the decision and surface what HE would want.

opts.text: Apply substituted judgment using his prior statements; offer an ethics consultation if the family conflict persists
opts.rat: #3 — Reach a values-based decision, using the ethics committee to mediate intractable conflict.

opts.text: Once a comfort-focused plan is chosen, change code status to DNR and write the comfort-care/withdrawal plan with anticipatory symptom orders
opts.rat: #4 — Operationalize the decision with the orders needed for a safe, comfortable withdrawal.

opts.text: Carry out the comfort-focused terminal extubation with family present and ongoing bereavement support
opts.rat: #5 — Execute the plan, prioritizing comfort, presence, and support.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Support the wife as legal surrogate to voice what HER HUSBAND would want, using his prior statements (substituted judgment)
opts.rat: Keeps the decision anchored in the patient's values and respects the surrogate's proper role.

opts.text: Offer an ethics committee consultation to help resolve the disagreement between the wife and son and address futility
opts.rat: A fair, transparent process for conflict rather than unilateral clinician action.

opts.text: Pre-medicate with titrated opioids ± benzodiazepines and an antisecretory agent before and during extubation to ensure comfort
opts.rat: Anticipatory, titrated comfort medication prevents and relieves dyspnea, agitation, and secretions (double effect).

opts.text: Discontinue nonbeneficial interventions per the comfort plan — vasopressors, CRRT, routine labs, vital-sign checks, and alarms — and stop tube feeds per goals
opts.rat: Removing nonbeneficial, burdensome interventions aligns care with the comfort-focused goal.

opts.text: Provide chaplaincy, family presence, and bereavement support, and make an organ/tissue-donation referral to the OPO per policy
opts.rat: Holistic end-of-life care includes spiritual/emotional support and the legally required donation referral (notification only).

opts.text: Tell the family the team is 'withdrawing care' and there is 'nothing more we can do'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — we withdraw nonbeneficial TREATMENT while intensifying COMFORT; this language abandons the family and is inaccurate.

opts.text: Withhold ordered opioids during terminal extubation out of fear of hastening death
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — under the doctrine of double effect, comfort is the duty; withholding indicated symptom relief causes needless suffering.

opts.text: Let the son unilaterally overrule the wife and demand continued aggressive treatment the patient would not have wanted
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the son does not outrank the spouse-surrogate, and the patient's known wishes govern.

opts.text: Pressure or guilt the surrogate toward a particular choice to speed the decision
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — coercion violates autonomy; the nurse informs and supports without imposing a choice.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish the legal surrogate and ensure the prognosis is communicated clearly and compassionately
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Identify who decides and make sure the decision will be informed.

opts.text: Facilitate the interdisciplinary family meeting, reframe around HIS values (substituted judgment), and offer an ethics consult for the conflict
opts.rat: #2 — Bring the team and family together, focus on the patient's wishes, and mediate disagreement.

opts.text: Once a comfort-focused goal is agreed, obtain DNR and comfort-care/withdrawal orders with anticipatory symptom-management medications
opts.rat: #3 — Translate the decision into the orders required for a safe, comfortable withdrawal.

opts.text: Prepare for terminal extubation: stop vasopressors/CRRT/labs/alarms, premedicate for comfort, manage secretions, position the patient, and bring the family to the bedside
opts.rat: #4 — Set up the environment and the patient for a comfortable, dignified withdrawal with family present.

opts.text: Perform the terminal extubation, titrate comfort medications to relieve any distress (double effect), and provide continuous presence and bereavement support
opts.rat: #5 — Execute the withdrawal with comfort as the priority and sustained support for the family.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each action or outcome as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse helps the wife voice what HER HUSBAND would have wanted, keeping the focus on his values rather than the family's preferences
opts.rat: This is substituted judgment applied correctly — the surrogate speaks for the patient.

opts.text: An ethics consult is offered when the son and wife remain in conflict, rather than one clinician deciding unilaterally
opts.rat: The ethics committee provides a fair process for intractable conflict and futility questions.

opts.text: Before extubation the patient receives titrated morphine and an antisecretory agent and appears calm with no air hunger or distress
opts.rat: Anticipatory, titrated comfort medication achieving symptom control is the goal of a comfort-focused withdrawal.

opts.text: The team explains they are withdrawing nonbeneficial TREATMENTS while intensifying COMFORT, and continues to bathe, reposition, and attend to the patient
opts.rat: Care is never withdrawn — only nonbeneficial treatment; comfort, hygiene, and presence continue.

opts.text: The nurse withholds ordered opioids during extubation because she worries they might hasten death, and the patient develops visible air hunger and grimacing
opts.rat: ⚠️ Under the doctrine of double effect, comfort is the duty; withholding indicated symptom relief causes needless suffering.

opts.text: The team proceeds with aggressive treatment the patient explicitly never wanted because the son insists, overriding the wife and his known wishes
opts.rat: ⚠️ The son does not outrank the spouse-surrogate, and the patient's known wishes govern the decision.

opts.text: Staff tell the family 'there's nothing more we can do' and stop comfort medications and routine hygiene
opts.rat: ⚠️ Abandoning comfort care and using hopeless language violates the duty to care; only nonbeneficial treatment is withdrawn.

opts.text: A nurse with a conscientious objection quietly leaves without arranging coverage, briefly leaving the patient unattended during the withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Conscientious objection requires advance notice, a competent replacement, and continuity of care — never abandonment.

opts.text: After extubation the patient dies peacefully within hours with family present, no signs of distress, and bereavement support provided
opts.rat: A comfortable, dignified death consistent with the patient's wishes, with the family supported, reflects the goal of care being met.
